questions.stem: A 70-year-old man presents with a 3-week history of rapidly progressive, diffuse erythema and scaling covering 90% of his body surface area. He reports malaise, fever, and tender cervical lymphadenopathy. He was started on amoxicillin for a chest infection 4 weeks ago and has no personal or family history of skin disease. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The acute onset after a new drug, fever, and lack of prior skin disease favour a drug reaction. Sézary syndrome typically has a more insidious course and severe pruritus, but can mimic drug eruptions; the temporal link with amoxicillin is discriminating.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with a 10-year history of chronic plaque psoriasis presents with a 2-day history of widespread bright erythema and sheets of superficial pustules, accompanied by high fever and malaise. She is tachycardic and feels systemically unwell. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The presence of sheets of pustules on an erythematous background, with systemic upset, in a patient with known psoriasis, is classic for pustular psoriasis. Erythrodermic psoriasis typically lacks prominent pustulation, and AGEP is drug-related, not linked to psoriasis.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man with severe atopic dermatitis since childhood presents with erythroderma, widespread lichenification, and generalized lymphadenopathy. He reports intense pruritus but no fever. Skin swabs are negative for bacteria. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The history of long-standing atopic dermatitis and lichenification favours erythrodermic atopic dermatitis. Sézary syndrome can mimic but usually has a later onset, more severe pruritus, and typical haematological features (e.g., Sézary cells), and lacks the chronic eczema background.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old man from a nursing home presents with a 2-month history of progressive, diffuse erythema, scaling, and severe pruritus. He has a past history of chronic venous eczema. On examination, you note hyperkeratotic plaques on his hands and feet, and burrows in the interdigital web spaces. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The presence of burrows is pathognomonic for scabies. Crusted scabies in the elderly can present with generalized erythroderma and hyperkeratosis, mimicking other causes. The lack of a drug history and the classic burrows discriminate it from drug reactions or lymphoma.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with stable plaque psoriasis, well-controlled on topical calcipotriol/betamethasone, stops using his treatment for 6 weeks. He now presents with progressive, diffuse erythema and fine scaling over 80% of his body area. He feels otherwise well, with no fever or pustules. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The history of psoriasis and withdrawal of topical therapy leading to gradual widespread erythema and scaling without pustules is typical of erythrodermic psoriasis. Generalized pustular psoriasis would feature pustules; drug reaction would have temporal link to a new drug, not withdrawal.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with hypertension (on hydrochlorothiazide) presents with sudden onset generalised erythema covering 95% of body surface area, fever (38.2°C), and mild pruritus. No prior skin disease. Which single blood test result is most suggestive of a drug-induced aetiology?
questions.explanation: Eosinophilia is a common feature of drug-induced hypersensitivity syndromes. Elevated LDH and CRP are non-specific; elevated alkaline phosphatase and troponin are not typically associated with drug-induced erythroderma.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man presents with widespread erythema. Which clinical finding is diagnostic of erythroderma?
options: Erythema affecting more than 90% of body surface area
questions.explanation: Erythroderma is defined as erythema and scaling affecting more than 90% of the body surface area. Fever and lymphadenopathy are associated features but not diagnostic.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old patient with erythroderma becomes hypotensive (BP 85/50 mmHg) and oliguric (urine output <0.3 mL/kg/h). Which laboratory result best confirms the presence of skin failure?
questions.explanation: Skin failure leads to loss of protein via scaling skin, causing hypoalbuminaemia (often <30 g/L). Other abnormalities are secondary or non-specific.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with chronic erythroderma for 6 months develops worsening dyspnoea and bilateral pitting oedema. Which investigation best confirms the suspected complication?
questions.explanation: Chronic erythroderma can cause high-output cardiac failure due to increased cutaneous blood flow. Echocardiography demonstrates increased cardiac output, confirming the diagnosis.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old HIV-positive man (CD4 200 cells/μL) on antiretroviral therapy presents with erythroderma and axillary lymphadenopathy. Which investigation is most likely to establish the underlying cause?
questions.explanation: In HIV patients, erythroderma may be due to primary cutaneous lymphoma, drug reaction, or infection. Skin biopsy with immunohistochemistry is the first-line diagnostic test to exclude lymphoma.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man presents with a 3-week history of rapidly spreading erythema and scaling covering 85% of his body surface area. He has no prior skin disease. Skin biopsy confirms psoriasis. He is systemically well, afebrile, with normal renal function and no lymphadenopathy. What is the most appropriate first-line therapy?
questions.explanation: Methotrexate is first-line systemic therapy for erythrodermic psoriasis. Systemic corticosteroids (prednisolone) are contraindicated due to risk of pustular flare. Ciclosporin can be used but is not first-line unless rapid response needed. Acitretin and etanercept are reserved for failure of first-line agents.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with chronic plaque psoriasis presents with acute-onset erythroderma, fever (38.5°C), tachycardia, and dehydration. He has not responded to topical therapy. What is the most appropriate initial systemic therapy?
questions.explanation: Ciclosporin has a rapid onset of action (within 1-2 weeks) and is preferred in acute severe erythrodermic psoriasis with systemic upset. Prednisolone is avoided due to risk of pustular psoriasis. Methotrexate and acitretin are slower. Infliximab is second-line.

questions.stem: A 2-year-old child with severe atopic dermatitis presents with erythroderma covering 90% body surface area, with oozing and crusting. He is systemically well, with no fever. What is the most appropriate first-line therapy?
options: Oral prednisolone 2 mg/kg daily for 5 days
Topical potent corticosteroid (e.g., betamethasone valerate 0.1%) under wet wraps
questions.explanation: Intensive topical therapy with potent corticosteroids under wet wraps is first-line for erythrodermic eczema in children. Systemic steroids are avoided due to rebound risk. Methotrexate and ciclosporin are second-line. Phototherapy is not acute therapy.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman on carbamazepine for epilepsy develops erythroderma, fever (39°C), cervical lymphadenopathy, and eosinophilia (1.5 x 10^9/L). Liver enzymes are raised. Drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms (DRESS) is diagnosed. What is the most appropriate therapy?
options: Immediate rechallenge with carbamazepine at a lower dose
questions.explanation: Systemic corticosteroids are first-line for moderate-to-severe DRESS syndrome to control systemic inflammation. Rechallenge is contraindicated. Topical steroids are insufficient. IVIG and ciclosporin are reserved for refractory cases.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with known Sézary syndrome (erythrodermic cutaneous T-cell lymphoma) presents with worsening erythroderma and severe pruritus. He has failed topical therapy. What is the most appropriate first-line systemic therapy?
questions.explanation: Extracorporeal photopheresis is first-line systemic therapy for erythrodermic mycosis fungoides/Sézary syndrome. PUVA is used but not first-line for erythroderma. Systemic steroids are not disease-modifying. Methotrexate is second-line. Topical steroids are insufficient.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents to the emergency department with gradual onset of generalised erythema and scaling over three weeks, covering 80% of his body surface area. He is afebrile, heart rate 85 bpm, blood pressure 125/80 mmHg, and oxygen saturations 98% on room air. There is no prior history of skin disease. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Perform a punch biopsy of affected skin
Admit to hospital for observation and investigation
questions.explanation: In stable erythroderma, admission is indicated due to risk of metabolic complications and progression. Biopsy is important but not immediate; topical steroids may worsen if not targeted; systemic steroids are avoided without a clear cause.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with chronic plaque psoriasis abruptly stopped his methotrexate two weeks ago. He now presents with diffuse erythema and scaling covering 90% BSA. He is alert, but heart rate 110 bpm, blood pressure 100/65 mmHg, and skin is warm with loss of piloerection. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Administer intravenous normal saline fluid resuscitation
questions.explanation: Erythroderma can cause significant fluid loss and haemodynamic instability. Fluid resuscitation is the priority. Restarting methotrexate may be considered but not immediately; biopsy is not urgent; checking methotrexate level is irrelevant.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman presents with acute onset erythema covering her entire body over 24 hours, associated with fever of 39.5°C, confusion, and hypotension (80/50 mmHg). She has no known skin disease. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
questions.explanation: The patient has signs suggestive of staphylococcal toxic shock syndrome or sepsis. Immediate antibiotics are critical. Emollients and biopsy are secondary; dermatology consult can wait; lumbar puncture is not indicated.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with long-standing atopic dermatitis develops erythroderma. Over the past 24 hours he becomes increasingly breathless with orthopnoea, raised jugular venous pressure, and bilateral leg oedema. His heart rate is 100 bpm, blood pressure 105/70 mmHg. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
questions.explanation: Erythroderma can cause high-output cardiac failure; acute pulmonary oedema requires urgent diuresis. Echocardiography is confirmatory but not immediate; salbutamol for bronchospasm not indicated; chest X-ray can be done after starting treatment.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man with HIV (CD4 200 cells/µL, not on antiretrovirals) presents with rapid onset of generalised erythema and desquamation over 2 days. He started co-trimoxazole prophylaxis one week ago. He is haemodynamically stable. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
questions.explanation: Drug reaction is the most likely cause in this immunocompromised patient. Stopping the suspect drug is the first priority. Starting ART may exacerbate reactions; biopsy is secondary; IVIG is for specific conditions like toxic epidermal necrolysis; antihistamines are supportive.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with a 20-year history of chronic plaque psoriasis presents with a 2-week history of widespread erythema covering 90% of body surface area. He has no fever. Which single finding would most urgently suggest progression to a life-threatening variant?
questions.explanation: The presence of discrete pustules on an erythematous background in a patient with erythrodermic psoriasis indicates progression to generalised pustular psoriasis, a life-threatening emergency. Joint pain (psoriatic arthritis), nail pitting, Koebner phenomenon, and scalp scaling are features of chronic plaque psoriasis without systemic danger.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman presents with acute-onset erythroderma, facial oedema, and fever (38.5°C) two weeks after starting allopurinol for gout. She has no past dermatological history. Which single finding would most strongly suggest a diagnosis of drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms (DRESS) rather than a simple drug eruption?
questions.explanation: Peripheral eosinophilia >1.5 × 10⁹/L is a hallmark of DRESS and is rare in simple drug eruptions. While lymphadenopathy and transaminitis can occur, eosinophilia is the most specific red flag for systemic involvement. Pruritus and papules are non-specific.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old man with long-standing eczema presents with erythroderma. On examination, his skin is hot and red, but his core temperature is 35.2°C. He is alert. Which single finding warrants immediate escalation to intensive care?
questions.explanation: Hypothermia (core temperature <35°C) in erythroderma indicates failure of thermoregulation due to excessive heat loss from dilated cutaneous vessels, predisposing to arrhythmias and metabolic complications. Tachycardia and mild hypotension are common; normal respiratory rate and saturations are reassuring.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man presents with a 3-month history of progressive erythroderma, severe pruritus, and has lost 5 kg weight. He has bilateral axillary and groin lymphadenopathy (nodes 2 cm). Which single finding on investigation would most strongly suggest that the erythroderma is due to cutaneous T-cell lymphoma (Sézary syndrome)?
options: Peripheral blood smear showing cerebriform lymphocytes
questions.explanation: Cerebriform lymphocytes (Sézary cells) in the peripheral blood are diagnostic of Sézary syndrome, a leukemic form of cutaneous T-cell lymphoma. Elevated IgE and eosinophilia are seen in atopic dermatitis; ANA is non-specific; thrombocytosis is reactive.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman develops acute erythroderma with fever and hypotension (BP 80/50 mmHg) one day after a vaginal delivery. She had a vaginal swab positive for group A Streptococcus. Which single finding is the most likely life-threatening complication of this presentation?
questions.explanation: The triad of acute erythroderma, fever, and hypotension in a postpartum woman with group A Streptococcus is classic for streptococcal toxic shock syndrome. High-output cardiac failure complicates chronic erythroderma; SSSS is staphylococcal and usually in children; AGEP is drug-induced; erythrodermic psoriasis is subacute.
